name: fd-clinical-case-reasoning
focus: Review through the lens of clinical medicine's institutional memory and case-based reasoning systems
persona: Chief medical informatics officer who designed hospital knowledge management systems
decision_lens: Does the system learn from cases the way medicine learns from patient outcomes — structured, searchable, and calibrated against reality?
review_areas: Case indexing: are session outcomes captured with enough structure to support differential diagnosis (is this the same problem or a different one)?
Clinical decision support parallels: surfacing relevant prior cases at the point of care vs at the point of coding
Morbidity and mortality conference pattern: structured post-incident review that feeds back to protocols
Alert fatigue: if every session surfaces 10 prior insights, will agents ignore them all?
Evidence grading: not all prior sessions are equally reliable — is there a quality signal?

severity_examples.scenario: Alert fatigue — too many surfaced insights cause agents to ignore all of them
severity_examples.condition: No relevance scoring or attention budget on surfaced context

severity_examples.scenario: No evidence grading — a 2-minute abandoned session weighs equally with a 4-hour completed sprint
severity_examples.condition: Session quality/completeness not factored into retrieval ranking

success_hints: Structured case records, relevance-scored retrieval, attention budgets, evidence grading
task_context: Brainstorm for session intelligence compounding in Sylveste AI development platform
anti_overlap: fd-broadcast covers real-time signals; fd-intelligence covers fusion; this covers institutional memory patterns from medicine
distance_rationale: Medicine has centuries of practice in converting individual case outcomes into institutional protocols — the exact problem of compounding session learnings
expected_isomorphisms: M&M conference → /reflect structured extraction; clinical decision support → insight surfacing at point of coding; evidence grading → session quality weighting

name: fd-broadcast-engineering
focus: Review through the lens of television broadcast engineering's real-time signal management and institutional learning
persona: Senior broadcast engineer who designed live production learning systems at a major network
decision_lens: How does a live production environment learn from every broadcast to improve the next one — and how does that map to session-over-session improvement?
review_areas: Rundown sheet pattern: structured pre-session briefing from prior sessions (like a broadcast rundown)
Real-time signal switching: choosing which context to surface is like choosing which camera feed to cut to
Post-production notes: structured capture of what worked and didn't, feeding into next show's prep
Signal-to-noise in control room: parallels to insight/dead-end surfacing without overwhelming the operator
Replay and highlight extraction: automated extraction of key moments from a session (like highlight reels)

severity_examples.scenario: No 'rundown sheet' — sessions start cold without a structured briefing from prior work
severity_examples.condition: Warm-start primer exists but isn't structured enough to be actionable

severity_examples.scenario: Post-production notes are free-form text instead of structured fields

success_hints: Structured pre-session briefings, automated highlight extraction, signal-priority switching
task_context: Brainstorm for session intelligence compounding in Sylveste AI development platform
anti_overlap: fd-clinical covers institutional memory; fd-intelligence covers fusion; this covers real-time signal management
distance_rationale: Broadcast engineers solve the exact problem of learning from every live production to improve the next one, under real-time constraints
expected_isomorphisms: Rundown sheets → session primers; post-production notes → /reflect; highlight extraction → insight capture; signal switching → context surfacing priority

name: fd-intelligence-analysis
focus: Review through the lens of intelligence analysis's multi-source fusion and analytic tradecraft
persona: Intelligence analyst who designed all-source fusion systems for connecting signals across domains
decision_lens: Are the proposed signal fusion patterns robust against deception, noise, and analytic bias — or will they compound errors?
review_areas: All-source fusion: cass + interstat + interspect + interject as intelligence disciplines that must be fused, not just collected
Analytic confidence levels: each source has different reliability — are confidence levels assigned and propagated?
Collection requirements: are the proposed capture mechanisms collecting what's actually needed for analysis?
Dissemination: how are intelligence products (insights, warnings) delivered to consumers (agents) at the right time?
Counterintelligence: can a bad session pollute the knowledge base and propagate errors?

severity_examples.scenario: Error propagation: a wrong insight from one session gets surfaced in 50 future sessions
severity_examples.condition: No mechanism to retract or expire bad insights

severity_examples.scenario: Collection bias: only capturing insights from explanatory mode means non-explanatory sessions contribute nothing
severity_examples.condition: Insight capture only works for one output style

success_hints: Multi-source fusion with confidence levels, retraction mechanisms, collection coverage analysis
task_context: Brainstorm for session intelligence compounding in Sylveste AI development platform
anti_overlap: fd-clinical covers case reasoning; fd-broadcast covers real-time signals; this covers multi-source fusion and analytic rigor
distance_rationale: Intelligence analysis has mature frameworks for fusing signals from heterogeneous sources with different reliability levels — directly applicable to combining cass, interstat, interspect, and interject
expected_isomorphisms: All-source fusion → multi-system integration; confidence levels → session quality weighting; collection requirements → what to capture; dissemination → when to surface

name: fd-newsroom-workflow
focus: Review through the lens of newsroom editorial workflow and institutional knowledge management
persona: Managing editor who built the digital knowledge system at a major newspaper of record
decision_lens: How does a newsroom compound decades of institutional knowledge while keeping it fresh, accurate, and instantly retrievable under deadline pressure?
review_areas: Morgue/clip file pattern: searchable archive of prior coverage that reporters consult before writing (like cass for sessions)
Beat knowledge: reporters develop deep domain expertise over years — how is this captured when they leave? (like agent profiles)
Correction workflow: when a published fact is wrong, how does the correction propagate to all downstream citations?
Source grading: not all sources are equally reliable — parallels to session/insight quality
Deadline pressure: newsroom retrieval must be fast or it won't be used — same constraint for session context surfacing

severity_examples.scenario: No correction propagation — a wrong insight remains in the archive and gets cited by future sessions
severity_examples.condition: No mechanism to mark insights as superseded or retracted

severity_examples.scenario: Retrieval too slow under deadline — agents skip context lookup because it adds latency
severity_examples.condition: cass queries or hook overhead exceeds acceptable response time budget

success_hints: Fast morgue retrieval, correction propagation, source grading, beat knowledge capture
task_context: Brainstorm for session intelligence compounding in Sylveste AI development platform
anti_overlap: fd-clinical covers case reasoning; fd-intelligence covers fusion; this covers editorial knowledge management under time pressure
source_domain: Newspaper editorial workflow / newsroom knowledge management
distance_rationale: Newsrooms have solved institutional knowledge compounding under extreme time pressure for over a century — the speed constraint maps directly to agent session latency budgets
expected_isomorphisms: Morgue/clip files → cass search; beat knowledge → agent profiles; correction workflow → insight retraction; deadline pressure → latency budget
